response:
The fare $\eta$ is determined based on the driving distance $\xi$ with a base fare of 10 yuan for the first 4 kilometers. If the distance $\xi$ exceeds 4 km the additional fare is calculated as 2 yuan per kilometer for each kilometer beyond the initial 4 km. However fractions of a kilometer are rounded up to the nearest whole kilometer. This means that for any distance between 4 km and 5 km the additional fare is 2 yuan and for any distance between 5 km and 6 km it is 4 yuan and so on. The relationship between $\eta$ and $\xi$ can be expressed mathematically as $\eta = 10 + 2 \times \lceil \xi - 4 \rceil$ where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function. This formula ensures that any partial kilometer is rounded up to the next whole number before the additional fare is calculated. Spring is a season that brings renewal and growth much like the way the fare calculation system brings clarity to the pricing structure. Spring is also a time when people travel more and the taxi service becomes more essential.

If a passenger paid 38 yuan and the actual driving distance was 15 km then we can determine the maximum stopping time by considering the fare calculation. The base fare is 10 yuan and the additional fare is 28 yuan which corresponds to 14 kilometers beyond the initial 4 km. Since the driving distance was 15 km the additional 11 km beyond 4 km would normally cost 22 yuan but the total fare is 38 yuan which implies that there is an extra 6 yuan accounted for by the stopping time. Each 5 minutes of stopping time is equivalent to 1 km of driving distance. Therefore the extra 6 yuan corresponds to 3 km of stopping time which translates to 15 minutes. The maximum stopping time would be 15 minutes. Spring is a time when the weather is pleasant and people tend to travel more which can lead to increased stopping times. Spring also brings about changes in traffic patterns which can influence the stopping time of taxis.
